subsystem: ImageMagick JPEG 2000 (JP2/J2K/JPT) encoder coder. WriteJP2Image() is the writer entry point that translates an in-memory MagickCore Image into a JPEG 2000 codestream via the OpenJPEG (libopenjp2) encoder API. It reads user-supplied encoding options (resolutions, quality, rate, progression order, sampling factor, tile geometry), builds an opj_cparameters_t and per-component opj_image_cmptparm_t descriptors, populates an opj_image_t with pixel samples, then drives opj_start_compress/opj_encode/opj_end_compress over an opj_stream_t.
provenance: Identified from the ImageMagick coder idioms: MagickBooleanType/ExceptionInfo/ImageInfo/Image types, MagickCoreSignature asserts, OpenBlob/CloseBlob/GetVirtualPixels/GetPixel* accessors, ThrowWriterException, GetImageOption/GetImageProperty, and the OpenJPEG API surface (opj_cparameters_t, opj_image_cmptparm_t, opj_image_create, opj_setup_encoder, opj_start_compress, OPJ_CLRSPC_* / OPJ_CODEC_* constants). The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2700_vuln */' and file name train_0268_cwe665.c confirm this is the WriteJP2Image function from ImageMagick coders/jp2.c.
trust_boundary: This is an ENCODER (write path), but multiple inputs it consumes are attacker-influenceable. (1) The image OPTIONS read via GetImageOption(image_info, ...) — 'jp2:number-resolutions', 'jp2:quality', 'jp2:rate', 'jp2:progression-order' — and image_info->sampling_factor / image_info->extract are strings that can originate from a command line, a coder invoked on untrusted input, a web/API front end, or an -define passed through by a service that transcodes user content. Their length and content are not bounded by this function. (2) image->columns, image->rows, image->depth, image->colorspace, image->alpha_trait come from a previously decoded (possibly untrusted) source image. The hostile fields are therefore the option strings driving the tcp_distoratio[]/tcp_rates[] fill loops and numresolution, the sampling_factor string driving subsampling_dx/dy, and the image geometry driving arithmetic and buffer sizing.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature of the JPEG 2000 writer. image_info carries encoding settings and user-supplied options/defines; image is the source raster (dimensions, depth, colorspace, alpha, pixels); exception is the MagickCore error channel populated on failure. It returns MagickBooleanType (MagickTrue on success, MagickFalse on failure). This is the coder registered for the JP2/J2K/JPT magick tags, so it is reachable whenever a caller asks ImageMagick to write those formats.

The local declarations. `parameters` is an OpenJPEG opj_cparameters_t compression-parameter struct that lives ON THE STACK and internally contains fixed-size arrays tcp_rates[100] and tcp_distoratio[100] (plus scalar fields like numresolution, tcp_numlayers, subsampling_dx/dy). `jp2_info[5]` is a fixed 5-element array of per-component descriptors (precision, bpp, subsampling, width, height). `channels` is the component count (1, 3, or 4) that indexes both jp2_info[] and, later, jp2_image->comps[]. `i` is a single register ssize_t reused as the loop index across every loop in the function — the resolution loop, both option-parsing loops, and the channel/pixel loops — which makes the guard placement in those loops security-relevant. `jp2_codec`, `jp2_image`, `jp2_stream` are OpenJPEG handles allocated later.

Standard MagickCore preamble: signature asserts validate the caller-supplied handles (compiled out in NDEBUG builds, so they are debugging aids not security gates), optional trace logging of the filename, then OpenBlob opens the destination for binary writing. If the blob cannot be opened the function returns early with MagickFalse. No untrusted parsing yet.

opj_set_default_encoder_parameters initializes the whole parameters struct (including subsampling_dx/dy to 1 and the tcp_* fields). The for-loop derives a default wavelet resolution count by finding the smallest i in [1,6) where 2^(i+2) exceeds both image dimensions; if neither triggers, the loop exits with i==6. numresolution is set to that bounded value. Then the 'jp2:number-resolutions' option, if present, OVERRIDES it with StringToInteger(option) — a fully user-controlled integer with no range clamp before it is handed to OpenJPEG's encoder setup.

Seeds the rate-control parameters: one quality layer, rate[0]=0 meaning lossless, distortion allocation on. If a non-default quality (not 0 and not 100) is requested, the first distortion ratio slot is set from image_info->quality and fixed-quality mode is enabled. These are single-index writes to element 0 and are safe on their own; they matter because the later option loops re-fill the same arrays.

If image_info->extract is set, it is parsed as an absolute geometry string into a RectangleInfo, and the parsed width/height/x/y populate the JP2 tile parameters (cp_tdx/cp_tdy/cp_tx0/cp_ty0) and enable tiling. Note cp_tdy is first assigned geometry.width and only replaced by geometry.height when the HeightValue flag is present — a functional quirk (tile height defaults to width) rather than a memory-safety issue. Values are cast to int and passed to OpenJPEG, which will validate tile sizes.

Parses the 'jp2:quality' option as a comma-separated list of PSNR floats, writing each into parameters.tcp_distoratio[i]. The loop's CONDITION performs the write (sscanf into tcp_distoratio[i]) and only AFTER entering the body does it test the index bound with `if (i > 100) break;`. It then advances p past the next comma. On exit, tcp_numlayers is set to i+1. tcp_distoratio is a 100-element array (valid indices 0..99), so the interaction between the write-in-condition and the `> 100` guard is the crux of the buffer-safety analysis for this block.

Maps the 'jp2:progression-order' option string to one of the five OpenJPEG progression-order enum values via exact case-insensitive comparison. Unrecognized strings leave the default order untouched. This is a bounded enum selection with no memory-safety exposure.

The rate variant of the same pattern: parses 'jp2:rate' as comma-separated floats into parameters.tcp_rates[i], with the write again performed in the loop CONDITION and the bound test `if (i >= 100) break;` inside the body after the write. tcp_rates is likewise a 100-element array (valid 0..99). tcp_numlayers is set to i+1 and disto_alloc enabled. The guard here uses `>= 100` rather than `> 100`, which changes the exact off-by-one behavior but does not eliminate the out-of-bounds condition write.

If a sampling factor string is present it is parsed as two decimal integers into subsampling_dx and subsampling_dy (the return value is discarded, so a partial/failed parse can leave one or both at their default or uninitialized-by-this-line value). An image 'comment' property, if present, is aliased into cp_comment (a const-cast into the encoder parameters). subsampling_dx/dy are later used as DIVISORS when computing pixel destination offsets, which makes their attacker-supplied values (including 0) security-relevant.

Determines the component count and colorspace from the source image. Default is 3 channels / sRGB. YUV maps to SYCC with dx=2. Grayscale maps to 1 channel / GRAY. Otherwise the image is transformed to sRGB. An alpha trait adds one channel. So channels is 1, 3, or 4 (gray+alpha would be 2). Multi-component transform (MCT) is enabled only for exactly 3 channels. The maximum channels value is 4, which bounds all subsequent jp2_info[]/comps[] indexing against the 5-slot jp2_info array.

Zero-initializes the 5-slot jp2_info array, then fills the first `channels` descriptors with per-component precision (from image->depth, bumped by 1 for 1-bit JPT/JP2 to satisfy OpenJPEG), sign=0, subsampling dx/dy (copied from parameters), and width/height. Because channels <= 4 and the array has 5 slots, this loop is in-bounds. The dx/dy copied here are the same attacker-influenced subsampling divisors used in the pixel loop.

Creates the OpenJPEG image object with `channels` components described by jp2_info and the chosen colorspace; on allocation failure it throws. It then sets the image canvas offsets/extent (x0/y0/x1/y1) — the x1/y1 computation multiplies (columns-1)/(rows-1) by subsampling_dx and casts to unsigned int. Cinema-profile compliance is applied for specific 12-bit DCI dimensions. Finally the alpha flag is set on the appropriate trailing component: comps[3] for 4-channel, or comps[1] for gray+alpha (channels==2). The comps[] indices used (3, 1) are consistent with the channels branch that selected them.

The pixel conversion nest: for each row it fetches image->columns virtual pixels, then for each column and each component computes a scale factor and a destination pointer q into comps[i].data. The destination index is (y/dy * columns/dx + x/dx), i.e. it DIVIDES by comps[i].dy and comps[i].dx (the subsampling factors). The switch stores the scaled channel sample (gray/red, alpha/green, blue, alpha) as an int. p is advanced by GetPixelChannels per column, and per-row progress is reported. The correctness of the index arithmetic and the non-zero-ness of dx/dy are the safety-critical properties here; the writes go directly into OpenJPEG-allocated component buffers.

Selects the codec by magick tag (JPT/J2K/JP2), installs warning/error handlers, and calls opj_setup_encoder with the fully-populated parameters and image. This is the point at which numresolution, tcp_numlayers, and the tcp_rates/tcp_distoratio arrays are consumed by OpenJPEG. A stream is created and its user I/O callbacks and user data (the MagickCore image blob) are wired; on stream-create failure the codec/image are destroyed and the function throws. Compression runs via start/encode/end; on any failure all three handles are destroyed and the writer throws. On success the handles are destroyed, the blob closed, and MagickTrue returned. The cleanup paths are symmetric and free each allocated handle exactly once.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds STACK write. The loop CONDITION writes sscanf's parsed float into parameters.tcp_distoratio[i] and is evaluated BEFORE the body's bound check. tcp_distoratio is a fixed 100-element array (valid indices 0..99). The guard `if (i > 100) break;` first fails to fire until i reaches 101, and even then the write for that iteration has already happened in the condition. Concretely: when i==100 the condition writes tcp_distoratio[100] (already OOB) and `100 > 100` is false so the loop continues; when i==101 the condition writes tcp_distoratio[101] (OOB) and only then does the guard break. An attacker who controls the 'jp2:quality' option supplies a comma-separated list of 101+ float tokens (e.g. '1,1,1,...') and overruns the opj_cparameters_t struct on the stack, corrupting adjacent parameter fields and potentially the saved frame. The invariant the code must uphold is: check `i` against the array capacity BEFORE the sscanf write (i.e. bound the index prior to writing, and use a strictly-less-than-100 limit).

Off-by-one out-of-bounds STACK write in the 'jp2:rate' twin of the previous loop. tcp_rates is a fixed 100-element array (valid 0..99). Because the write into tcp_rates[i] happens in the loop CONDITION and the bound test `if (i >= 100) break;` runs only afterward in the body, the iteration where i==100 first WRITES tcp_rates[100] (one past the end) via sscanf and only then breaks. An attacker controlling the 'jp2:rate' option with 101 comma-separated float tokens triggers a single 4-byte OOB write past tcp_rates into the following struct field. The fix is to bound-check i before performing the sscanf write, with a limit of 99 (last valid index). This is a distinct anchor from the distoratio loop because the guard operator differs (`>=` vs `>`) and the destination array is different, though the root cause (write-in-condition, check-after) is shared.

Unvalidated resolution count. StringToInteger parses an arbitrary signed integer from the 'jp2:number-resolutions' option with no clamp to OpenJPEG's legal range (1..OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS, i.e. 33). A negative value, zero, or a value far above the maximum is passed straight into opj_setup_encoder at line 297. Depending on the linked libopenjp2 version this can cause the encoder to compute decomposition-level tables based on an out-of-range count, leading to rejected encode, excessive allocation, or indexing errors inside the library. The invariant: numresolution must be validated to a sane, in-range positive value before being handed to opj_setup_encoder.

Potential division by zero. The sampling_factor string is parsed into subsampling_dx/dy with the sscanf return value discarded, and no check that the resulting factors are non-zero. Those factors are copied into jp2_info[i].dx/dy (lines 197-198) and then used as DIVISORS in the pixel-destination index at lines 245-246: `y/jp2_image->comps[i].dy` and `x/jp2_image->comps[i].dx`. If an attacker supplies '0,0' (or '0' for the first field) the divisor becomes 0 and the per-pixel arithmetic performs an integer division by zero — undefined behavior that on common platforms raises SIGFPE and crashes the process (denial of service). The invariant: subsampling_dx and subsampling_dy must be validated to be >= 1 before they reach the division.

Destination-pointer arithmetic into the OpenJPEG component buffer relies on the subsampling divisors and on comps[i].data being sized to hold columns*rows/(dx*dy) samples. If dx/dy are attacker-set to values inconsistent with how opj_image_create sized comps[i].data, or if the (y/dy*columns/dx + x/dx) computation does not match the library's own component sizing, q can point outside the allocated data buffer and the subsequent `*q = ...` becomes an out-of-bounds heap write. Additionally `1UL << comps[i].prec` shifts by a precision derived from image->depth (+1 for 1-bit): a prec of 64+ would be undefined-behavior shift, though depth is normally small. The invariant: the index formula must agree exactly with OpenJPEG's component buffer allocation for the same dx/dy/w/h, and dx/dy must be the same non-zero values used at allocation time.

Unsigned/int overflow in canvas-extent computation. columns and rows come from the source image and can be very large; multiplying (columns-1) by subsampling_dx (also user-influenced) and casting to unsigned int can overflow or truncate, producing an x1/y1 that is inconsistent with the component dimensions the encoder was told about. A mismatched canvas extent can cause OpenJPEG to mis-size or mis-walk the tile/component grid. This is a lower-severity integrity issue than the stack overwrites but should be bounded; the invariant is that the computed extent must not overflow unsigned int and must remain consistent with w/h in jp2_info.

summary: This is ImageMagick's WriteJP2Image OpenJPEG encoder. The dominant risk is two attacker-controlled option-parsing loops ('jp2:quality' and 'jp2:rate') that write sscanf results into the fixed 100-element tcp_distoratio[]/tcp_rates[] arrays of a stack opj_cparameters_t with the bound check placed AFTER the write, yielding out-of-bounds stack writes when the option string carries 100+ comma-separated values. Secondary risks are an unvalidated numresolution from a user option and an attacker-supplied sampling factor of zero that becomes a division-by-zero (and buffer-sizing mismatch) in the pixel loop.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry-point signature; the risk lives in the option-derived data it processes, captured by the specific findings below.
Stack struct containing fixed arrays tcp_rates[100]/tcp_distoratio[100] that the option loops overrun.
Fixed 5-slot buffer paired with the channels count; per method a fixed buffer + count must be dispositioned. Bounded safe (channels<=4) but documented.
Count is derived to 1/2/3/4 from colorspace+alpha and stays < 5, so it cannot overrun jp2_info[] or comps[].
Reused loop index; each loop re-initializes it, so cross-loop reuse is not itself a bug (the guard placement inside the option loops is the real issue, captured separately).
Bounded loop producing numresolution in [1,6]; safe on its own.
User-controlled, unclamped integer fed to opj_setup_encoder.
Write-in-condition with post-write `i>100` guard overruns the 100-element array; OOB stack write.
Same write-in-condition pattern; `i>=100` guard still permits an OOB write at index 100.
Unchecked user factors become divisors; zero causes division by zero and can desync buffer sizing.
Bounded enum selection; unrecognized strings are ignored, no memory effect.
Parsed geometry populates int tile parameters validated later by OpenJPEG; the cp_tdy=width default is a functional quirk, not a memory-safety issue.
Const-cast alias of a comment string into the encoder params; lifetime is fine within this call and it is not length-copied here.
Multiply-and-cast of large dimensions by subsampling can overflow unsigned int, desyncing canvas extent.
Index formula divides by attacker-influenced dx/dy and must match OpenJPEG's own component sizing or it writes OOB in the component heap buffer.
comps[] indices are gated by the exact channels value that guarantees those components exist.
Handle allocation and error-path cleanup are symmetric; each handle is destroyed exactly once, no double-free or leak on the shown paths.
Debug-only sanity checks, compiled out under NDEBUG; not a security control.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds stack write in 'jp2:quality' PSNR parse loop (tcp_distoratio[100])
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (index bound checked after the write; off-by-one/over-index)
findings.invariant: The array index must be validated against the capacity of tcp_distoratio (100 elements, max valid index 99) BEFORE sscanf writes tcp_distoratio[i]; the loop must stop at i==99.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker sets the 'jp2:quality' image option to a string of 101 or more comma-separated float tokens. Because sscanf writes tcp_distoratio[i] in the loop condition and the `if (i > 100)` test runs only afterward, the iterations at i==100 and i==101 write past the end of the 100-element array, corrupting adjacent fields of the stack opj_cparameters_t (and potentially beyond). Impact ranges from encoder misbehavior to stack memory corruption / potential control-flow influence.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm sizeof(parameters.tcp_distoratio)/sizeof(float) == 100 in the linked openjpeg.h, and that no earlier caller clamps the token count; verify the write occurs in the loop condition before the guard.

findings.title: Off-by-one out-of-bounds stack write in 'jp2:rate' parse loop (tcp_rates[100])
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one out-of-bounds write (write-in-condition precedes bound check)
findings.invariant: Index i must be validated (< 100) before sscanf writes tcp_rates[i]; the loop must not perform the write for i==100.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker sets 'jp2:rate' to 101 comma-separated float tokens. The iteration at i==100 first writes tcp_rates[100] (one element past the end) via the sscanf in the loop condition, and only then does `if (i >= 100) break;` fire. This is a single 4-byte OOB write past tcp_rates into the following opj_cparameters_t field on the stack, corrupting encoder parameters.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm tcp_rates has exactly 100 elements and that the guard runs after the condition write; verify no upstream bound on the number of rate tokens.

findings.title: Unvalidated user-supplied resolution count passed to encoder
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation (unclamped count into library API)
findings.invariant: numresolution must be clamped to OpenJPEG's legal range (1..OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS) before opj_setup_encoder consumes it.
findings.failure_mode: The 'jp2:number-resolutions' option is parsed by StringToInteger with no bounds check, so a negative, zero, or very large value is stored into parameters.numresolution and handed to opj_setup_encoder. Depending on libopenjp2 version this can cause rejected encoding, oversized allocation, or out-of-range decomposition-level indexing inside the encoder.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether the linked OpenJPEG validates numresolution internally; if not, this is a direct out-of-range parameter into encoder setup.

findings.title: Division by zero from attacker-supplied sampling factor in pixel loop
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0268_cwe665.c (WriteJP2Image), divisor use at lines 197-198 and 245-246
findings.invariant: subsampling_dx and subsampling_dy must be validated to be >= 1 before they are copied into jp2_info[].dx/dy and used as divisors in the pixel index computation.
findings.failure_mode: The sampling_factor string is parsed with the sscanf result discarded and no zero-check. A value of '0,0' sets subsampling_dx/dy to 0, which are copied into comps[i].dx/dy and then used as divisors in q=comps[i].data+(y/dy*columns/dx+x/dx) at lines 245-246, causing integer division by zero (SIGFPE / undefined behavior) and a process crash — a denial of service. Non-zero but mismatched factors can also desync q from the library's buffer sizing.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm subsampling_dx/dy have no other clamp before the divide, and that a zero factor reaches lines 245-246 for at least one component.

findings.title: Component-buffer pointer arithmetic depends on subsampling and library sizing
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0268_cwe665.c (WriteJP2Image); comps[i].data is allocated inside OpenJPEG (opj_image_create)
findings.invariant: The (y/dy*columns/dx + x/dx) index must never exceed the number of samples OpenJPEG allocated for comps[i].data, which is derived from the same dx/dy/w/h; dx/dy must be the exact non-zero values used at allocation.
findings.failure_mode: The destination index divides by the attacker-influenced subsampling factors and multiplies by image->columns rather than the component's own subsampled width. If dx/dy or the arithmetic disagree with OpenJPEG's internal component sizing, q lands past comps[i].data and *q performs an out-of-bounds write into the heap-allocated component buffer. Also note `1UL << comps[i].prec` at line 244 is UB if prec >= 64.
findings.what_to_confirm: Compare this index formula against how opj_image_create sizes comps[i].data for the same dx/dy/w/h; confirm columns vs comp width usage matches.

findings.title: jp2_info[5] fixed array bounded by channels count (defensive contract)
findings.anchor: opj_image_cmptparm_t jp2_info[5]; ... for (i=0; i < (ssize_t) channels; i++) jp2_info[i]....
findings.bug_class: Fixed buffer indexed by count (currently in-bounds)
findings.invariant: channels must remain <= 5 (the jp2_info capacity) for every code path that fills jp2_info[i] and creates the OpenJPEG image with `channels` components.
findings.failure_mode: As written, channels is derived to at most 4 (3 base + 1 alpha, or 1 for gray), so the loop at line 185 stays within the 5-slot jp2_info array — no overflow today. The finding documents the fixed-buffer/count contract per review method: any future change that lets channels exceed 5 (e.g. a new colorspace path adding components) would overflow jp2_info on the stack and mis-size opj_image_create. No exploit on the current code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm every path setting channels keeps it <= 5, and that opj_image_create is always called with the same channels used to fill jp2_info.

audit_checklist: Confirm tcp_distoratio and tcp_rates are each exactly float[100] in the linked openjpeg.h and that both parse loops (lines 108, 145) can write index 100/101 because the bound check runs after the sscanf in the loop condition.
Add a strict pre-write bound (i < 100) to both the 'jp2:quality' and 'jp2:rate' loops so no write occurs at index 100 or beyond.
Clamp parameters.numresolution from StringToInteger (line 67) to OpenJPEG's legal range (1..OPJ_J2K_MAXRLVLS) before opj_setup_encoder.
Validate subsampling_dx/dy from image_info->sampling_factor (lines 159-160) are >= 1 before they are copied to comps[i].dx/dy and used as divisors at lines 245-246 (avoid SIGFPE).
Verify the pixel destination index (y/dy*columns/dx + x/dx) never exceeds the sample count OpenJPEG allocated for comps[i].data for the same dx/dy/w/h.
Check that channels can never exceed the 5-slot jp2_info[] capacity on any colorspace/alpha path.
Check the x1/y1 extent computation (lines 207-210) for unsigned int overflow with large columns/rows and non-1 subsampling.
Confirm the sscanf return value on line 159 being ignored cannot leave subsampling_dx/dy in an unintended state relative to the defaults set by opj_set_default_encoder_parameters.
